Okay, so I'm looking at this problem where a mutation in the DateTimeZone class caused several tests to fail. The mutation was made in lines 287-293 of the file, specifically altering how the offsetFormatter is created and handled.

First, let me understand what the original code does. The offsetFormatter() method is a static synchronized method that checks if cOffsetFormatter is null. If it's null, it creates a new DateTimeFormatterBuilder, appends a timezone offset with certain parameters, and then builds the formatter. This formatter is stored in cOffsetFormatter for future use.

The mutation changed this logic. It removed the initialization part inside the if block and just put a semicolon there. So now, when cOffsetFormatter is null, it does nothing except return null or some default? Wait, no—the code after the mutation doesn't show an assignment, so I think the formatter isn't being created anymore.

This would mean that any code relying on offsetFormatter() to provide a proper DateTimeFormatter would fail. That's likely why the tests started failing—because when they tried to use this formatter, it wasn't initialized correctly.

Looking at the test results, several TestDateTimeZone tests went from passing to failing. These tests probably depend on the correct behavior of the offset formatter. For example, if the formatter isn't created, methods that rely on it might not format dates and times as expected, leading to assertion failures in these tests.

On the other hand, one test, TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear, went from failing to passing. Maybe this was because the mutation didn't directly affect how month names are handled, or perhaps that specific test wasn't relying on the formatter and started working for a different reason, but that seems less likely. More probably, it's just an unrelated change, but since other tests failed, I'm focusing on the DateTimeZone issues.

The key here is that by removing the initialization of cOffsetFormatter inside offsetFormatter(), any code that uses this method will either get null or not have a properly initialized formatter. This leads to incorrect date-time formatting and comparisons in various parts of the system, causing the tests to fail.

So, for the mutant_id "src-main-java-org-joda-time-DateTimeZone-290-1", which corresponds to the mutation in line 290 (or around there), I need to determine if this mutation is adjacent to a fault. The concept of "fault adjacency" here refers to whether the mutation directly caused the failure or if it's part of a larger issue.

In this case, yes, the mutation directly affects the initialization of cOffsetFormatter. Without this formatter being correctly set up, multiple parts of the system that rely on it would fail. Therefore, the mutation is indeed adjacent to the fault—it directly caused the test failures by breaking a critical component.
